ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ebi tempura don; mapped to broader 'rice bowl dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for ebi tempura don; A0CKP (rice-based dish with seafood) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese rice bowl preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of ebi tempura don is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with 3 pieces of battered deep-fried shrimp and tempura dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Ebi Tempura Don — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ebi Tempura Don (cr_id F05100029). All per-100g macros and available micronutrients are taken from this record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shrimp tempura (NDB No. 35174) and white rice composite, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ebi tempura don as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (jasmine/short-grain), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The tempura batter and sauce contribute minor carbohydrate fractions that do not materially alter the GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 95.44) / 100 ≈ 68.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 23.86 × 4 = 95.44 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 171 mg/100 g; tempura dipping sauce adds significant additional sodium at point of consumption
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 171 mg/100 g is below the 200 mg/100 g threshold per 100 g, but a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 684 mg sodium before sauce; patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side, use it sparingly, and limit portion size to one bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 23.86 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 0.48 g dietary fibre/100 g drives rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base has a high glycaemic index (~72) and the full serving delivers approximately 95 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate with minimal fibre buffering; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish, substitute brown rice if available, and request tempura sauce on the side to reduce added sugar and glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes saturated fat and potential trans fats from frying oil; sodium load from sauce is an additional cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying the shrimp tempura adds saturated fat and oxidised lipids that are unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, request sauce on the side to reduce sodium intake, and consider grilled or sashimi alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; refined white rice base adds pro-inflammatory glycaemic load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried tempura is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE generation and pro-inflammatory lipid oxidation; patients with RA should avoid this dish and consider tendon don or sashimi don as lower-inflammation alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi tempura don (海老天丼, ebi tendon) is a classic Japanese donburi rice bowl dish consisting of a generous serving of steamed short-grain white rice topped with three pieces of ebi (shrimp or prawn) tempura and drizzled with a sweet-savoury tempura dipping sauce (tsuyu) made from dashi, soy sauce, and mirin. The shrimp are coated in a light, airy tempura batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp, creating a contrast of crunchy batter and tender, juicy prawn against the soft, sticky rice. The dish is a staple of Japanese teishoku restaurants and donburi chains and is widely available in Singapore's Japanese restaurant scene. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 162 kcal, 4.85 g protein, 4.93 g fat, and 23.86 g carbohydrate with only 0.48 g dietary fibre, reflecting its white rice base and deep-fried protein component. Sodium is 171 mg/100 g from the base dish, but the tempura sauce adds substantial additional sodium at point of consumption. The dish has a high glycaemic index (~72) driven by the white rice base and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or rheumatoid arthritis. It is moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat from frying. Common allergens include crustaceans, gluten (batter and soy sauce), egg, and soy.
common_user_queries: Is ebi tempura don suitable for diabetics?
Is ebi tempura don high in sodium?
Can I eat ebi tempura don if I have hypertension?
What is the GI of ebi tempura don?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100029, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The dish is a well-defined Japanese donburi with a standardised composition. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy shrimp tempura data and documented accordingly. GI is estimated from the white rice dominant carbohydrate component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.85 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; it is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for shrimp tempura (NDB 35174) and white rice composite, weighted by approximate dish composition (70% rice, 25% shrimp tempura, 5% sauce). This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB or USDA measured value when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Japanese rice bowl dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05100029, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine/short-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for ebi tempura don as a composite dish has been published in an approved source. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional Japanese tempura batter may contain mirin (alcohol-containing) and the dish is prepared in non-halal-certified Japanese restaurants in most contexts. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311069 mapped as broader ancestor; no species-specific or preparation-specific FoodOn term for ebi tempura don exists as of 2026. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest analogous category.
